questions.q: Mordell–Weil says that for an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the group $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is:
questions.explain: The theorem gives a decomposition $E(\mathbb{Q}) \cong \mathbb{Z}^r \oplus T$ where $T$ is a finite torsion group and $r \ge 0$ is the rank.

questions.q: By Mazur's theorem, how many isomorphism classes of torsion subgroups $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ are possible?
questions.explain: Mazur (1977): the torsion is one of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ for $n\in\{1,\ldots,10,12\}$ (11 groups) or $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2n$ for $n\in\{1,2,3,4\}$ (4 groups), total 15.

questions.q: For the congruent-number curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - 25x$, the rank is $1$ and the torsion is $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$. How many rational torsion points does $E(\mathbb{Q})$ contain (including $O$)?
questions.explain: $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ has order $4$; the non-identity $2$-torsion points are $(0,0), (5,0), (-5,0)$ plus the identity at infinity.

questions.q: BSD's original numerical heuristic predicts that $\prod_{p \le X} \dfrac{N_p}{p}$ grows like:
questions.explain: Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer observed on EDSAC that the product grows like $c\cdot(\log X)^r$, where $r$ is the rank of $E(\mathbb{Q})$.

questions.q: Here $N_p = \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$. Higher rank corresponds to curves having, on average:
choices: no points at all for most $p$
questions.explain: Heuristically, lots of rational points forces many points modulo $p$. This bias in $a_p = p+1-N_p$ (systematically negative in some average sense) is the statistical shadow of positive rank.

questions.q: By Hasse's bound, $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$. For $p = 25$ this would give $|a_p| \le ?$ (take $p$ as a real number here, ignoring that $25$ is not prime).
questions.explain: $2\sqrt{25} = 10$. The Hasse bound controls each factor $N_p/p = 1 - a_p/p + 1/p$ so it lies in $[1 - 2/\sqrt{p} + 1/p,\ 1 + 2/\sqrt{p} + 1/p]$.
BSD equates the analytic rank (order of vanishing of $L(E,s)$ at $s=1$) with the algebraic rank of the Mordell–Weil group.

questions.q: For a rank-$0$ elliptic curve, BSD predicts that $L(E, 1)$ equals zero ($\to$ enter $0$) or is nonzero ($\to$ enter $1$)?
questions.explain: Rank $0$ means $\operatorname{ord}_{s=1} L(E,s) = 0$, i.e. $L(E,1) \ne 0$. Kolyvagin's theorem proves the BSD implication in this direction for modular $E$.

questions.q: The full BSD conjecture expresses $\lim_{s\to 1} \dfrac{L(E,s)}{(s-1)^r}$ in terms of which invariants?
The Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ satisfies which scaling law?
questions.explain: Tate's limiting construction makes $\hat h$ exactly quadratic, so $\hat h(nP)=n^2\hat h(P)$. Equivalently, $\langle P,Q\rangle = \tfrac12(\hat h(P+Q)-\hat h(P)-\hat h(Q))$ is bilinear.

questions.q: Let $E(\mathbb{Q})$ have rank $2$ with height-pairing Gram matrix $\begin{pmatrix}2 & 1\\ 1 & 3\end{pmatrix}$ on a basis. Compute the regulator $R=\det G$.
questions.explain: $R = 2\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 1 = 5$. Positive-definiteness (so the pairing is a genuine inner product) requires $\det G > 0$, which holds here.

questions.q: By convention, the regulator of a rank-$0$ elliptic curve is:
questions.explain: The free part is zero-dimensional, so the Gram matrix is $0\times 0$ and its determinant is $1$. This keeps refined BSD sensible when $r=0$: the formula reduces to $L(E,1) = \Omega\cdot |\Sha|\cdot\prod c_p / |E_{\text{tors}}|^2$.

questions.q: An element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ corresponds geometrically to:
choices: a torsor $C/\mathbb{Q}$ for $E$ with points over every $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and $\mathbb{R}$ but no point over $\mathbb{Q}$
questions.explain: $\Sha = \ker\bigl(H^1(\mathbb{Q},E) \to \prod_v H^1(\mathbb{Q}_v,E)\bigr)$ classifies principal homogeneous spaces for $E$ that are everywhere locally trivial but globally nontrivial — failures of the Hasse principle.

questions.q: If $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is finite, Cassels–Tate forces its order to be a perfect square. Which of $\{1, 2, 4, 6, 9\}$ is NOT a possible value of $|\Sha|$? Enter the value.
questions.explain: $6$ is not a perfect square, so it is forbidden by the Cassels–Tate pairing (which gives $\Sha$ an alternating non-degenerate pairing, hence square order). $1, 4, 9$ are squares; $2$ can occur as $|\Sha[2]|$ but not as $|\Sha|$ — however the question only asks about non-square obstruction, making $6$ the cleanest answer.

questions.q: The $n$-descent sequence reads $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_n(E) \to \Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})[n] \to 0$. If $|\mathrm{Sel}_2(E)| = 32$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E(\mathbb{Q})| = 8$, then $|\Sha(E)[2]|$ is:
questions.explain: From the exact sequence, $|\Sha[2]| = |\mathrm{Sel}_2|/|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E| = 32/8 = 4$. This is the standard way $2$-descent pins down $|\Sha[2]|$ even when the full group is out of reach.
